subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
title: International Maritime Organization (IMO) and Key Conventions
focus: IMO's structure, membership, and major maritime conventions like SOLAS, MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention.
rationale: This is a distinct institutional topic covering IMO basics and its associated conventions, which are highly testable for UPSC Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is a UN specialized agency responsible for shipping safety and preventing marine pollution. Established via a 1948 Geneva convention (in force 1958), it has 176 members, including India. Its highest technical body is the Maritime Safety Committee (MSC). Key conventions include SOLAS (1974), MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention (2009) for safe and environmentally sound ship recycling.
evidenceBullets: IMO is a UN specialized agency for shipping safety and marine/atmospheric pollution prevention.
India is one of the 176 Member States of the IMO.
The Maritime Safety Committee (MSC) is the IMO's highest technical body.
Key conventions include SOLAS (1974), MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention (2009) for ship recycling.

subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
title: Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU) and Ocean-based CCUS
focus: Technologies for capturing CO2 (DAC, industrial) and utilizing it (EOR, fuels, chemicals), plus ocean-based storage methods.
rationale: Covers the DST roadmap, CCU pathways, and ocean-based CCUS techniques. The intersection of Science & Tech and Environment makes it a prime area for conceptual questions.
sectionExcerpt: Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU) involves capturing CO2 from industrial sources or via Direct Air Capture (DAC) and using it as feedstock. Direct utilization includes Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) and industrial uses, while CO2-to-Products involves converting CO2 into chemicals (urea, plastics), fuels (methanol via Fischer-Tropsch), and mineral carbonates (cement). Ocean-based CCUS includes Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) and Ocean Fertilisation to boost CO2 absorption.
evidenceBullets: The Department of Science and Technology (DST) launched India's first R&D Roadmap for Net Zero through CCUS.
Direct Air Capture (DAC) isolates CO2 directly from ambient air using a gas trapping system.
Direct Utilisation of CO2 includes Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) and food/healthcare applications.
CO2-to-Products includes conversion to methanol/fuels via Fischer-Tropsch synthesis and mineral carbonates for building materials.
Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) accelerates CO2 absorption by adding pulverized minerals like lime.
Ocean fertilisation promotes phytoplankton growth by adding micronutrients like phosphorus and nitrogen.

subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
title: Methane Emissions and the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC)
focus: Methane's properties as a GHG, major emission sources, and the role of the CCAC.
rationale: Methane is a critical Short-Lived Climate Pollutant (SLCP). The CCAC's mandate and India's emission status are prime UPSC targets.
sectionExcerpt: The Global Methane Status Report 2025 was released by UNEP and the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC). The CCAC, founded in 2012 within UNEP, targets short-lived climate pollutants (methane, black carbon, HFCs, tropospheric ozone); India joined in 2019. Methane is the second biggest driver of climate change, over 80 times more potent than CO2 over 20 years, but persists for only 7-12 years. 60% of emissions are human-driven (agriculture, fossil fuels, landfills), while wetlands are the largest natural source. India is the 3rd largest emitter.
evidenceBullets: The Global Methane Status Report 2025 was released by UNEP and the CCAC.
CCAC (founded 2012, convened within UNEP) targets short-lived climate pollutants: methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.
India joined the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) in 2019.
Methane is over 80 times more potent than CO2 over a 20-year period but persists for only 7-12 years.
60% of methane emissions are anthropogenic; wetlands are the largest natural source.
India is the world's 3rd largest methane emitter, primarily driven by stubble burning.

subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
title: Glacier Preservation and Glacial Loss
focus: Recent declarations of dead glaciers and the environmental consequences of glacial melting.
rationale: Specific environmental events (dead glaciers) and geographical consequences (albedo, sea level, GLOFs) form a distinct, testable cluster.
sectionExcerpt: Nepal's Yala Glacier was recently declared dead after shrinking 66% since the 1970s. Other dead glaciers include Pizol (Switzerland), Clark (US), Ayoloco (Mexico), and Okjokull (Iceland), which had the world's first glacier funeral in 2019. Consequences of glacial loss include reduced albedo effect (increasing heat absorption), a 2 cm global sea level rise since 2001, loss of freshwater (glaciers store nearly 75% of Earth's freshwater), and increased risks of Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and avalanches.
evidenceBullets: Nepal's Yala Glacier was declared dead after shrinking 66% since the 1970s.
Okjokull glacier in Iceland had the world's first glacier funeral in 2019.
Glacial loss reduces the albedo effect, leading to increased heat absorption.
Melting glaciers have contributed nearly 2 cm to global sea level rise since 2001.
Glaciers store nearly three-quarters of Earth's freshwater.
